name: Project launch
goal: Leave the first month with aligned sponsors, a written scope everyone points at, a shippable slice in users’ or stakeholders’ hands, and a clear decision on what happens next.
description: Four-week arc: align and assign ownership, nail scope and measures of success, build and demo something real, then close with feedback and a staged rollout or pause. Dates shift to start from the day you create this plan.

tasks.title: Kickoff: charter, audience, and who decides
tasks.content:
First working session should end with a one-page charter, not just slides.

Cover in the meeting:
- Problem, intended audience or customer, and what “launched” means in plain language
- Sponsor / product owner: who can say yes to scope and tradeoffs
- Working team vs consulted-only; expected hours per week if people are partial
- Communication: where decisions live (doc, ticket system, Slack channel) and meeting rhythm
- Top five risks and assumptions—no more than one slide each

Before:
- Agenda + pre-read 24–48 hours ahead; link to any existing research
- Assign a notetaker; notes and actions same day

Done when:
- Charter draft exists with goal, sponsor, core team, and communication plan
- Open questions are listed with owners and target answer dates

tasks.title: Scope package: in/out, milestones, dependencies, success metrics
tasks.content:
Turn opinions into a short spec people can disagree with constructively.

Write down:
- Must-haves vs should-haves vs won’t-do (this version)
- Milestones with dates: design lock, build complete, internal demo, external pilot or launch
- Dependencies: other teams, data, legal, infra, vendors—with named contacts
- How you’ll know it worked: 2–4 measurable signals (adoption, latency, support volume, revenue proxy—whatever fits)
- Cut line: what you drop if the timeline slips two weeks

Process:
- Walk the sponsor through the doc; capture edits in the same file
- Explicit written approval (email or comment) so scope doesn’t drift by hallway chat

Done when:
- One canonical doc or epic description linked from the project home
- Success metrics and milestone dates are visible to the whole core team

tasks.title: Build the thinnest vertical slice and test it internally
tasks.content:
Ship something that crosses the stack or workflow end-to-end, even if ugly—integration risk is the point.

Define “done” for this slice before coding:
- User or stakeholder action that proves the path works
- Quality bar: which bugs block demo vs can be noted as follow-ups

Execution:
- Daily or every-other-day sync for blockers only; longer updates async
- Feature flag or environment so incomplete work doesn’t destabilize others

Internal demo:
- 20–30 minutes live walkthrough with Q&A
- Capture feedback as tickets tagged launch / post-launch / out of scope

Done when:
- Demo happened with core stakeholders present
- Feedback backlog exists; critical defects have owners and dates

tasks.title: External touchpoint, launch decision, and next 30 days
tasks.content:
Either put the slice in front of real users / a pilot customer or run a structured stakeholder review—whichever matches your risk.

Choose one path:
- Pilot: narrow cohort, success criteria from your scope doc, feedback channel
- Stakeholder sign-off: recorded demo + written go/no-go against metrics

Launch hygiene:
- Runbook: rollback, on-call, who communicates externally
- Support or CS briefed on what changed and known issues
- Analytics or logging verified so you can see the metrics you promised

Closeout:
- Short retrospective: what to keep, stop, and try next
- Updated roadmap or backlog for the next 30 days

Done when:
- Go/no-go is explicit; if go, date and owner for broader release are set
- If no-go, documented reason and either revised scope or project pause
